Quotation: I think I got it. I think the major factors are very similar for all programming languages. So, basically, you calculate risk, you calculate gains, you calculate the effort you have to put into it. The major triggers are safety; how can I say, the cut of external dependencies, I think that it doesn't matter if you are speaking about a Java or a Python program, you don't want to use unnecessary third-party libraries, which can be changed, which can be outdated, which can be not supported. That's a problem for Java, Python, or C++, also because, of course, the safety and the readability or the learnability of the codes are similar factors. I see the factors are the same, the thresholds are different, it's likely in safety is much more an issue in C++ than, let's say, in Java. And it might be that Python is even a higher issue than in C++ because Python is a more dynamic language with more problems coming out from runtime. And, for example, if you can use any new Python feature typings, if you can use typings as a new language feature to decrease the runtime problems to decrease the possibility of runtime box, that you will press it more than in C++ or in Java to introduce such features. So, I see that the major factors might be the same for every language. And the threshold is different. In C++, it might be that the performance is more important than in Java. Java is that slow anyway, it doesn't matter how you improve it, it's not so much gain. Actually, if you have a web application, a database application, you don't mind the performance at all. Not that much. Okay?

Quotation: The other trigger I also want to mention in C++ 11, is that before C++ 11, there was no standard multi-threading solution for C++. That doesn't mean that it wasn't multi-threading programming in C++, but you had to use third-party libraries, or you had to use non-standard features which might be platform-dependent. Replacing these features only gives you long-term guarantees that your code won't be corrupted. Either because the third-party library was outdated or just not supported anymore, or you don't need to bother with porting the features for new platforms. So, this is typically multi-setting. Not my project, but currently, I'm working on a project in which one of the major issues is renewing or refactoring the code, or modernizing the C++ code, which used exactly those third-party libraries. Again, before C++ 11 commercial libraries were being used instead of the standard parts, which were just not there. You know, C++ 11 also introduced several standard libraries, like regular expression libraries. Before that, there were no regular expression libraries, you had to go for third-party libraries. And there were, again, the maintenance issues, the no compatibility issues. It was 10 years ago, and that project, only now, decided to replace these libraries. You see that it wasn't something urgent, but sooner or later, they decided ”Okay, that's enough” it took too much work to maintain these third-party libraries.

For example, with Python, the language stopped receiving maintenance, it ends up not being purely an issue of being able to add new features, but also an issue of not receiving any patch if they find a security flaw in this interpreter version, and you end up having no choice but to update.

Quotation: You can track it, but it all depends on the project. It all depends on the project's maturity. It depends on who provides maintenance since there is a cost to keep up with it. As I mentioned, I work on a project that is still on Python 2. It is a project that comes from 2010, and there are few people in my work maintaining it. We are aware that it is risky to use a discontinued language version, but due to the effort it would take to update the dependencies to a newer version using Python 3, we decided to prioritize other things. It is a trade-off situation. It depends a lot on the context of the project you are working on. This project that I am working on in Kotlin, for example, is a much more recent project. It is a project that started early last year, so it is a little over 12 months old. For us, having the flexibility to change that, is less cumbersome. We can do that. I would say that the leaps in technology are not that great.

Quotation: First, to be aligned to the language’s platform itself, to not make the code [degrated], to not become outdated to the point there is no longer compatibility between versions. Did you notice, for example, that Google no longer allows the hosting of apps with outdated libraries on its Play Store platform? It means that if you make an app, you will always need to keep maintenance on the libraries that it references, update them constantly, otherwise you are banned from Google's application hosting platform. This is a classic example that code outdating implies the unfavorable issue of using the software itself, the language, and so on.

Concepts: Preventing System Obsolescence

Quotation: For untested code, it’s not refactoring, but reengineering. When you say reengineering, you terrorize the one in charge of managing. When they say: let’s refactor it, I’ll refactor it - the guy in management will think that it’s something with no risks involved. Right? But this is only possible if you already have tests; if you don’t, it’s not refactoring. Also, if you look at Martin Fowler’s book, the one who created refactoring, and wrote the book that originated this practice, his principle is: you need to have tests. If there are no tests, it’s not refactoring, you’re (reengineering it) [00:16:53], counting on all your experience and ability in order to perform the change. And I’ll tell you: I’ve screwed a system three weeks before the election, by just modifying a single line. I saw a line, and thought: what, why is this not a reference? And then put the reference there, one line. We performed the tests, everything alright, it passed the test. We locked the software, asked for the minister’s signature, all ministers signed it and everything, and when we got to more intense testing, the system went down. A single line. We had to call the secretary and say: Secretary, we need to call everyone back, as there’s an issue on the software that needs to be amended. In this one line change, there wasn’t a specific test to check the mess I’d made, and I did it because I thought it was going to work. When you don’t have tests, the risks are great, even for a single line. When there’s no test, I’m very scared to modify software, even with 25 years of experience. Actually, it’s precisely because of these 25 years of experience.
Codes: Relevance of regression/automated testing
Concepts: Availability of Automated and Regression Testing

Quotation: It depends a lot on the requisites, you know? For example, when we’re working on a system that’s already pretty old, we avoid refactoring features that are already under production, unless it’s strictly necessary. Or else, you would have to go through that whole process again to revalidate the implementation. So, we’ll update certain parts of the software, only the necessary ones. Nowadays, we can break down the software into services, it’s not that giant stack anymore, which was very difficult to change. Now, you can divide it into micro sets, and it helps a lot in maintaining and evolving the software. Because you can migrate and add new features only to parts where it’s safe to do it, you don’t need to do it as a whole.

Sometimes, keywords that didn’t exist before are added, so some methods that used these names start to fail too. So we rely on the IDEs, IDE analysis, Eclipse. Quotation: I’m not in my NameFirst, I might check it and might send you videos in which Google guys are speaking about that. They claim that they are using these kinds of automatic refactoring tools heavily and that I can do this. It’s possible to use their tools, but there are very important prerequisites for that, you have to have full coverage of the tests. Basically, our program proves it’s not possible on this industrial scale. After you refactor the code, you have to be certain that the behavior does not change or doesn't change negatively. The only way we can currently do this is by running regression tests. I think that I even saw Martin speaking about in his refactoring books, that the prerequisite of refactoring is an almost 100% test coverage because you just have to prove that the refactor code, that's the definition of the refactoring, behaves in the same way. And that's very rare. You rarely have it in an industrial project that one person does, it would be nice, it would be ideal, but in practice, It’s not that frequent. So, what I would say is that small syntactic changes are okay. In C++, for example, if from C++ 11, you can add the override keyword for richer functions, and that makes extra an insurance to avoid some nasty bug. To do this manually is a waste of time. If you have an automatic tool that can do this, it's almost trivial to detect what is a mutual function, and it's almost trivial to insert this overriding keyword, you hardly can mess up anything with that. That's okay, that's perfectly okay. But to refactor an inheritance or something like that, or changing smart, changing row pointers to smart pointers. That typically needs some more changes, sometimes the reorganization of your class, or your code is very hard to do automatically. It's a very high-level understanding of the code, the semantics of the code, no tool can do this at the moment. Well, you know, there are features like static analyzers or symbolic execution which we said 20 years ago were impossible, and now they are here. I can imagine that, let's say, in the next 10 years, both hardware and software will develop and that will be a possibility. It's definitely a good research area. But at the moment, or let's say in the next 5, 10 years, I don't see the highlight for you to apply this kind of tools, not only C++ but for other programming languages.
Codes: Relevance of regression/automated testing
Concepts: Availability of Automated and Regression Testing

Quotation: Most importantly, testing is in the first place. Not necessarily with unit testing or with integration testing, but the software must continue to function properly. Even if the goal is not to change the observable behavior, in order to increase readability or understanding, testing is necessary. Without testing, we don't even start this process. After implementing a test, you run the code and make the first change. You then repeat this incremental process known in software engineering. You run the test, modify the code line by line, getting the same result, until you finish the modification.
Codes: Keep the systems running and evolutive maintenances are more important than SCR efforts | Relevance of regression/automated testing

Quotation: The challenges. Well, it's usually the legacy code. That's the main issue. Because we have to do a refactoring, and it has to go through some tests. Sometimes you have to go through the whole QA again; if you don't have the unit tests, you have to be careful to do a refactoring of the unit tests. So, you have all these issues. So, as we have CI/CD nowadays, this has become easier, because you already write the tests in a way that it will run as soon as you modify, and the merge will only be done if this test passes on the pipeline side, on the side of the tool you are using to maintain your code. So, I think that was a nice touch, applying these pipelines on CI/CD. And the challenge is refactoring and maintaining this software, and not degrading it. Do a refactoring and use this to make your life easier, and don't get any bugs in the process.

Quotation:
K: So, from what you said, I assume that you have already used, or at least thought about using some tools like that to refactor...

P: Even developing such tools.

K: Oh, great. That's really great. Apart from your student’s project, at the end of the interview, we'd also like to hear about this idea, this ambition of yours. But today, according to you, there isn't any alternative that is safe or good enough for automatically rewriting large codes or industrial codes.

P: Yeah, because, in most cases, this does not involve (all) the syntactical changes, that involve semantical decisions.

K: So, would you say that the reason for the challenges or difficulties with those tools, and therefore the things that can block the usage in great masses of code on the codes that are high risk...

P: Blocking the refactoring tools?

K: Yeah. What could stop people from using them? You know, things that would make people insecure, or make them just not utilize them, and choose to rewrite manually the code when they had to do it?

P: Many factors. First of all, as I mentioned, it's very hard that you just start to rewrite the code, because you want to rewrite the code, you typically want to fix a bug or you want to modify some feature. So, and that's of course, won't be done by an automatic tool, anyway you have to do certain actions manually, and you modernize the code as a side effect. The other blocking factor is that C++ is an extremely complex language analysis, it’s much more complex than some of the other languages, let's say in Java, or C#, or similar. And to detect all the factors of where you have to use certain code to avoid any side effects, unwanted side effects is almost impossible at the moment. So, the refactoring tool is not mature enough to do this, and I'm not sure that they ever will be. It's not impossible, theoretically, but there are so many small issues that can put danger to the idea, which is just too tricky, let's say it this way.
Codes: Programming language complexity might hinder SCR efforts | SCR efforts may introduce bugs | SCR is a non-trivial effort
Concepts: Effort, Cost & Practical Constraints | Language Evolution Complexity & Instability | Risk, Uncertainty & Fear Factors

Quotation: This part gets into another sphere. The code developer does not own the code he writes, he has a major responsibility, but all that code is shared, unless you are a freelancer and you are selling your product, you hardly own the code. Usually, you can negotiate that. When you are working for a company on a project basis, even for your startup, you will have a trade-off that is your code maintenance, which is a very nice code and is time to market. It's a balance you have to keep between producing quality code while meeting the needs of a stakeholder, for example. There are countless times that you must make sacrifices, like quickly releasing a product to test and giving up some things, you're going to make a little more conservative code, for example, sometimes giving up some test scenarios because you have to validate an idea. We in the IT area, within software development, share our area of thought construction focused on software development, it is something that you can relate to, there is this constant exchange, and that is what will determine it. What makes us give this up is the project's need to include other people. There are techniques to overcome that, like creating technical departments, and never letting this get away from the discussions. That is what XP says about keeping transparency in what is happening. We know that we are giving it up and we will pay for it, and then at the right time, it will be agreed with developers, stakeholders, POs, and all these figures.

Quotation: video.js I don't know how I missed these but they're the same as the other example we reverted. I looked at how babel translates arrow functions and it at least doesn't do an extra `fn.bind()` so that's good. I want to make sure I'm not just being a luddite here so if there's fault in my reasoning let me know. I think it's an important issue. I still don't think the shortened syntax alone is benefit enough to warrant upping the javascript reading level for contributors. For most contributors JavaScript is probably still not their primary programming lang and we'd be replacing one of the most most well known keywords with one of the least known that does the exact same thing (in this specific case). Also Babel makes named functions when translating arrow functions. ``` var noTap = function noTap() {} ``` That goes against the "Arrow functions are always anonymous" rule and could potentially [create issues in IE8](http://kiro.me/blog/nfe_dilemma.html). Though I'm not sure how likely that is and I wish we could use named functions more for stack traces. Also if you have a text editor with a function shortcut there's not much benefit left over for this specific case. `f` then `tab` quickly in Atom makes an empty function. I do think there are some cool examples of using arrow functions like: ``` let square = x => x * x; ``` I would have a harder time arguing against that and it even seems more readable than the example above for some reason. And using it for it's `this` binding. ``` var boundFn = ()=> { this.foo(); } ``` But in all the examples here we seem to just be using it for it's shorter anonymous function syntax which if that's correct then for consistency there's over 500 other anonymous functions in the codebase we would need to update too. Am I missing another benefit in these examples?
Codes: Resistance to change | SCR might difficult code understanding | code standards might prevent source code rejuvenation efforts
Concepts: Human & Knowledge Factors | Standards, Governance & Process Constraints | Technical Debt & System Quality Constraints

Quotation: Four. in C++ 11, it was a huge step forward. It was a really large part, I have a lecture when I have a showcase, which shows that... I don't remember the correct numbers, but the size of the C++ standard originally was something like 800 pages. The C++ 11 standard was 1200, and currently, it's about 1600. So, it has doubled, which means that, partially, the language features (it says) [00:53:58] partially the libraries are doubled. So, the evolution is extremely fast, and to follow this, not only on the theoretical level, but on the level which I expect my program to be. So to apply these changes in practice, but at the same time still, be aware of the new traps and pitfalls. That's hard because it means you have to apply them, you have to try them out. You don't just read the books or the blogs, you have to apply them to existing problems and see the side effects. The problem is, if you have a new language feature, that's okay, everybody understands, if you have another new language feature, everybody understands that, but if you have, let's say, these two new language features, they sometimes interact. So, the complexity is not additive, but rather multiplicative, and the growth of the complexity. Imagine you have 100 new language features and all interact with it, or the others, you know, and that's where the complexity is, that even in the best effort, these language features always interact with each other, and that can have side effects, even such side effects, the standardization committee never thought. And there are debates, of course in the standardization committee under the conferences about us. So yeah, it's very hard, even for me, so I can imagine that those programmers who have daily work just to produce the code, and have fewer opportunities to do this. That's not easy.
Codes: Difficulty in following language evolution | Feature interactions create unexpected side effects
Concepts: Language Evolution Complexity & Instability | Technical Debt & System Quality Constraints

Quotation: It depends. Depends on the modification that you’ll make and the environment you’re in. If your environment values internal software quality, you can take your time and perform certain kinds of maintenance. Quotation: It's an important factor, but not the major drive. Usually, we are working with quite complex systems, and we don't refactor them unless we see a really important advantage from this point of view. Let's see a few examples: in C++, well-known issues if somebody is not carefully programming and accidentally happens that you can make memory leaks or different kinds of memory-related issues, that C++ is very well known about. And at Microsoft review, I can send you the reference, it claims that in the last 15 years, more than 80% of the security patches were related to memory issues. Okay, that's at last 15 years on average. Now, of course, when C++ 11 introduced smart pointers, and other techniques, which we use for the danger of these problems, we happily, and almost immediately started to rewrite the code using these new features. But this is almost exceptional, it's typical if you have a large industry code, which works more or less fine. If you have no if you don’t have something exception or trigger, you don't touch that part. First of all, much more danger comes from rewriting the code and introducing new techniques, which you don't want to take if you have no... if there’s nothing... okay, not nothing, but it's not an exceptional advantage you hope for. If you experience regular memory issues, okay? Which demands lots of time and effort to be fixed, of course. You’re happy, accept these new language features and change them. If you see new language features that solve something more elegantly, you say: “Ok, next time, I will use that, but I won’t touch my code. It's working, I have no time and resources to rewrite it. And I don't want to take the risk of a new box.”.

Quotation: This is a discussion that has no immediate answer. At Foton, I update the J2EE version, which will use newer versions, newer libraries, different frameworks. In this case, you have to redevelop. Sometimes you would not even need to redevelop some old code, but sometimes it is worth it. There are situations that we do. Now, when you have old libraries, cryptographic libraries that a client implemented, you use it the way it is, if it is working. When it's a big change, you will develop a new, more radical version of the product, and you will recompile, at least, to a more recent version. If not, you will redevelop a lot, but at least you will compile. Some things will be redeveloped, many others will be recompiled with newer versions. In the majority of cases, you upgrade when you need a library update. In general, you will not refactor an old code just because you changed the language to put a different interaction. The code is already tested and validated in production. It is not justifiable to take the risk, to increase cost and the risk of crashing in production for things that are already validated and structured. At most, you will recompile with a newer version. Some maintenances are structural, those at the architecture level and some maintenances are peripheral, such as screen and functionalities that use the same structure. For example, at Foton, we work a lot with the banking system, with banking transactions. So, for example, I have the SAC, deposit, statement, balance, transfer, and bill payment authorizers. If you have changed the authorization framework, you have a base that serves all authorizations. You will need to test all of them. But as a developer, as an architect, you will make that change in the base, in the higher classes, in the libraries, and you will validate with one or another functionality. Then, you will pass that on to the developers to make other changes. Then, when the system goes for a more complete test, you will validate the rest, but you will not validate the whole system, because sometimes you have 100 transactions, 100 features that use the same base class. There is a framework to know where you are working and what is impacted. But the focus is on the change you made, and you will not do everything over again. Each case is unique and has characteristics that sometimes you have to adapt to different application servers. For example, I developed with JBoss, and now I have to do the same system running on WebSphere, or Weblogic. That often doesn't affect all the functionalities. It affects your infrastructure a lot. It is much more at the architectural level. There are patterns that functionalities use. So if you validate and it worked, you believe the rest will also work. Then, you move on to the test team. You will need to do the full suite yourself. There are many cases, just like everything else in life.
Codes: Keep the systems running and evolutive maintenances are more important than SCR efforts
Concepts: Organizational & Managerial Constraints

Quotation: In lambda’s case, in my opinion, if it’s well used, you can get cleaner, more concise code, and as it’s cleaner, you also get better legibility. For example, in Java 7, in older systems it’s customary to declare an anonymous class with parameters. In my opinion, in legibility terms, it’s not the best way. And normally this anonymous class only needs to implement a method that you can transform into a functional interface so you can use the so-called lambda expression. Instead of creating an anonymous class that would have some 15 lines, you could reduce it to only 1, 3 lines. In the case of strings, it’s a bit more controversial in terms of legibility, as it will demand that the programmer knows the strings’ API. But it basically can simplify many manipulations in the sense of collections. An example that I’ve seen being used, and that I even used once, was for you to read an upload file. Instead of making many if-elses, many interactions inside of the same bytes list it has, you could use the string API and it turns out very concise, in my opinion. However, this is an API that requires a lot of caution, in terms of how you will mount it in the instructions sequence. This is a case that can affect legibility of code and make it harder to read than its older version. A functionality that would be very handy if I was using Java 8 is the interface’s default method itself. It would be like this: if you implement the interface, this class does not overwrite the code, and you set it to default mode, the default method itself would be used as a standard for the class. This significantly reduces the interface crash issues, and in case you want to refactor, for example, an abstract class that has some 200 other children that inherit from this abstract class, when you implement an interface with a new method that you created, you can declare the default and all the other classes will use this default mode as a standard, so you don’t need to implement the method in every single class. So, it would be mainly to avoid interface crashes.
Codes: Improve code expressiveness | Improve code understanding
Concepts: Code Readability and Expressiveness

Quotation: Well, in Java 8’s case, I think that one of the biggest benefits was it becoming less verbose. Before, we used to write many lines of code, if you had a comparator, or something like this, there was this need of creating an entire code to make that comparison, that ordering. With Java 8, you can do that in a lambda expression incorporated in a STR. So, what used to be ten lines before, you can reduce to one, eventually two. When there was the migration of C++98 to 2003, it was the same. C++ is highly verbose, so to declare a variable you had to write like, eighty characters sometimes, as the names were too long, so you had to write a lot of code, or copy and paste it. But then when C++2003 came, I think it was in 2011, you could set it to auto, a reserved word that references dynamic typing of that kind, so you don’t need to declare the variable’s name, as the type is already written and it will be identified dynamically. Hence, in my opinion, regarding these two languages, Java and C++, these last updates enabled us to write much less code, you know?

Quotation: Well, I think adopting new language features mostly introduces comprehensibility. I think your code becomes more understandable. For example, in Java, the try-catch block being replaced by the private resources makes it less error-prone. Also, using the auto keyword, you don't have to write a lot of template things; you only write the auto keyword and the compiler will understand what you meant. One more example: for performance updates or upgrades, using C++ 11 introduces the move constructor, which helps a lot with big projects with millions of lines of code, such as the ones we have. You’ll gain a lot of memory by decreasing consumption.

Quotation: Back to the challenges, if the application is quite stable, it's hard convincing yourself that it’s worthwhile to update just to get a new feature, when this could mean losing application stability. If it’s a new feature that helps you perform maintenance and an application that has just been started or is riddled with bugs, it’s worth it.
Codes: Improve code maintenance
Concepts: Maintainability and Testability impacts

Quotation: Okay. I see three or four major triggers to rewrite the code to modernize it. One which I mentioned is: when there is a new feature, which also has current and present danger, like memory leaks. Memory leaks are so common in C++ that it is worse to rewrite the code and replace the old techniques with newer ones, especially smart pointers in C++. Because they'll only spare the maintenance effort, and the effort you invest on rewriting the code comes back within a year. So, that's one point, one trigger.
Codes: Help on bug prevention
Concepts: Reliability and Correctness

Quotation:
K: Right. Anyway, we have two more scale questions, then we’re free for you to discuss the important things you want to discuss with us. So, the next question is, on the same scale from one to five, one is never, three is half of the time and five is always, how often would you say you implement the changes in the updates that you learned about the language in your existing code?

P: Well, if we touch the code, then we almost always try to use the new features. So, let's say four, but, as I mentioned, we don't touch the code just to modernize it. What usually happens is that we have a trigger, as we spoke about, to modify the code, and if we modify, of course, we always try to apply the newest features. But that might not be general for every developer in the industry, because I have been working with my Ph.D. students, very advanced guys themselves, language developers too. They are very sensitive to these new features. I can imagine that an average programmer does not apply the new features. Our average programmer can be on the scale two, for our programmers, let’s say four.

K: I see. So, for this question, I want to ask: how hard is it to keep updated on the new language features? I understand that it might not be as hard for you, since you are very, very internal to the languages, and you have to teach, you go to conferences, but how hard, would you say, from one to five? From one to five, where one is very easy, and five, it's very hard, almost impossible.

P: Four. in C++ 11, it was a huge step forward. It was a really large part, I have a lecture when I have a showcase, which shows that... I don't remember the correct numbers, but the size of the C++ standard originally was something like 800 pages. The C++ 11 standard was 1200, and currently, it's about 1600. So, it has doubled, which means that, partially, the language features (it says) [00:53:58] partially the libraries are doubled. So, the evolution is extremely fast, and to follow this, not only on the theoretical level, but on the level which I expect my program to be. So to apply these changes in practice, but at the same time still, be aware of the new traps and pitfalls. That's hard because it means you have to apply them, you have to try them out. You don't just read the books or the blogs, you have to apply them to existing problems and see the side effects. The problem is, if you have a new language feature, that's okay, everybody understands, if you have another new language feature, everybody understands that, but if you have, let's say, these two new language features, they sometimes interact. So, the complexity is not additive, but rather multiplicative, and the growth of the complexity. Imagine you have 100 new language features and all interact with it, or the others, you know, and that's where the complexity is, that even in the best effort, these language features always interact with each other, and that can have side effects, even such side effects, the standardization committee never thought. And there are debates, of course in the standardization committee under the conferences about us. So yeah, it's very hard, even for me, so I can imagine that those programmers who have daily work just to produce the code, and have fewer opportunities to do this. That's not easy.
Codes: Incremental SCR effort | Reactive approach
Concepts: Adoption Strategy and Timing | Execution Strategy and Effort Management
SubCategories: Execution and Operational Strategies | Strategic Decision-Making for Rejuvenation

Quotation: Yes. It's like I said, as we are now using DevOps a lot, this feature has been put in evidence. Since you're doing some refactoring, you're using a new version and everything, and you submit that for review, that will automatically run in our pipeline. Quotation: It also depends on the projects. For example, today, the projects that we work with are not up to date, they have many older versions’ frameworks, like two to three versions older. In this case, we need a detailed plan to evaluate the impacts, how much time would it take to migrate, if we have enough people to execute it, to test it, to put up an introduction. It depends a lot on the company. I have worked for three different companies here in Quebec, and the one I’m working for right now is pretty obsolete in terms of technology, so any migration process, or any new thing needs very detailed planning, containing the changes’ impact. Before this company, I was in another one that had a much better developed method. We worked on an 80/20 scale, being 80% for new features, and 20% for technical debt of the project. We always worked on the languages’ framework updates, performance improvement, error tracking, logs, this kind of thing. So, it depends a lot on the company’s working process.
Codes: Analyse costs/benefits before rejuvenate | Company/Team decision-making | Reducing technical debt
Concepts: Continuous Technical Debt Management | Decision-Making and Strategic Evaluation
SubCategories: Execution and Operational Strategies | Strategic Decision-Making for Rejuvenation

Quotation: I mean that, depending on the evolution, it won’t benefit the code at all, be it performance, readability, or something along these lines. Sometimes it could even cause some issues. So, you must analyze each case, to see whether an evolution is worthwhile to use in existent, or inexistent code.
Codes: Analyse costs/benefits before rejuvenate
Concepts: Decision-Making and Strategic Evaluation

Quotation: The third thing is, that if you have something with an extraordinary performance gate, performance is still important in certain projects. You know, if you have a Java project, or web application or database application performance, it is not that important, but if you are in investment banking and you are an automatic trading system, then 2% speedup can mean billions of dollars a year. Picture that. Or you just think about the Mars Rover, which was programmed in C++, 2% improvement in efficiency might mean that the solar energy is just enough for another two months. So, C++ is a specific language, which is mostly used when performance issues are coming into the discussion. I see certain situations when there are new C++ solutions, which are just performing better, and that can be an issue, but that is compared to the previous two walls. So, the standard stuff or the safety stuff is less important in many applications, you know, because there are special applications, of course, when this can be important, but in most situations, this is less important compared to other stuff. And, of course, there is, let's say, the force situation when you have more elegant, more readable solutions. And of course, typically, if you start to use those features, where you write new code, it might be used (there where)(then, when) [00:18:48] you touch the old code, but that hardly triggers the rewriting of the code. If there are any opportunities because you want to bring a new feature, or you want to fix a bug, then, perhaps, you don't only modify that part of the code, you change the source file, the whole source file. Let's say so, you set a bigger environment of the code, but it's just because it's more elegant, more readable. It's not typical that you touch the code.
Codes: Analyse costs/benefits before rejuvenate
Concepts: Decision-Making and Strategic Evaluation

And frequently, when you achieve the same behavior, the refactoring even becomes easier.

Quotation:
K: I see. What do you think of the tools that are more ambitious in their scope, but are not fully automatic? They suggest you a change that is more complex than just rewriting...

P: We are working on such a tool. For example, one of my Ph.D. students is working on a (so-called) [01:06:32] strong typing project. What is strong typing? You have a double floating-point number type in your code, it’s a variable, and it can express a length. Another variable is another same type variable, a double can represent a time. It's better not to mess up these two types, but if these are doubles, then there is always the chance that you just change them accidentally. Instead of passing a time, you pass lengths in a function parameter, and the compiler would complain because they are just topless. Those are very typical strings. You use strings for a name. A family name. (Perhaps) a hostname. At hostname cell family names are never mixed. But if they are strings, you can mess up the use of them. If you create a separate type for the hostname, you create a separate type for the family name, and you can write a safer code, our current programming languages only use strings for that. One of my experiences in my advanced C++ lecture was that... “Okay, guys, you know, the Doxygen library, this document generator library?, that typically uses things for our establishing. Start to refactor this just to change certain types. Pick up a variable that is, I don't know a class name and create a class name type for it, and then roll on to continue this.” What's the problem here? The problem here is that, of course, if you create a hostname type then somewhere you want to add a character for that, we want to concatenate two characters now, for strings you can concatenate, but in the hostname, there are no such features. You have to implement the concatenation and then you have to increment the output function, so it's a lot of work. Also, it can possibly be that somewhere you meet, you start from here with the hostname, you start from here with the family name and there is somewhere when the army is in a function or you call the same function with both the parameters. Then how to change the parameter of that function? Sometimes the human has to decide these things. We are going to have a manual tool that makes these detections and then offers you the choice on how to decide. Can we ever have a current project for this strong typing refactoring stuff? It's not to inject new language features instead of the old, but better to use the current language features to make stronger types. I believe in these tools, because I think that in many cases automatic decisions are either impossible or just not reliable, or not worth investing in because the human can decide it much better. And to support the human. That's important. Typically, you find a word, and then you ask whether to replace it or not. Okay, that's very (basic)(01:10:57) stuff in editors, and it's working fine.
Codes: Incremental SCR effort | Tool configurability influencing SCR adoption and control
Concepts: Execution Strategy and Effort Management | Tooling Constraints and Control Mechanisms
SubCategories: Execution and Operational Strategies | Tooling Support for Source Code Rejuvenation

Quotation: Normally, you have a number of patterns in your code. Quotation:
W: Alright. Could you tell me then, how did you benefit from these tools, for example, this IntelliJ one?

R: The main benefit, in my opinion, was on the code legibility itself, and for learning. Initially, I didn’t understand very well how lambda expressions worked, and I got to understand it better by using this tool. I could see it clearly: what you’re creating here, it can turn into this simpler expression. Also, I think it works as a kind of spark for you to understand what could be improved. For example, you didn’t know about lambda expressions and you’re coding on IntelliJ and it shows you this warning. From this, you could ask yourself, wait, what’s a lambda expression? I think this arouses the researcher’s curiosity and makes them want to search and know better about the feature. This way, it gets concise.
Codes: Reliance on static analysis to detect SCR opportunities
Concepts: Selective and Human-Controlled Use of Tool Support
SubCategories: Tooling Support for Source Code Rejuvenation

Quotation: Exactly. Clang is a static analysis tool, we are actually also developing static analysis tools, we are quite familiar with these tools. And there are, in Clang, the so-called modernizing tools which detect word constructs, which can be reflected automatically for new features. And there are two setups with these tools. One just warns you that “Hey, this is an old technique, which can be safely replaced with some new techniques.”, but also there is another version, or let's say, you give a flag, you can switch off the automatic refactoring stuff. So, that really changes the source code. Okay, so there are these kinds of refactoring tools, this Clang-Tidy library has these refactoring tools. And, actually, we are, ourselves, writing plugins for that. So, you know, this is a kind of pluggable infrastructure, there are lots of different possibilities. And this is more a framework, and you can develop different plugins to detect different possibilities and make the automatic change. So, we use that. But mostly, we just use manual rewriting. In most cases, these automatic refactoring tools can do nice, but pretty trivial changes. Major changes are not supported by tools. I don't want to say it's not possible, but on an industrial scale, I’m not aware of any C++ tools that are extremely good. This clang-tidy started to develop at Google, actually, and they are actively using it inside their codebase.
Codes: Tool configurability influencing SCR adoption and control
Concepts: Tooling Constraints and Control Mechanisms
SubCategories: Tooling Support for Source Code Rejuvenation

Quotation: Oh, yes, sure. I often use the assistant built into JetBrain's products, of course, they have a lot. But, for third-party applications, I barely use them for the reasons I mentioned before. I often try some third-party things to evolve my code. But, if I do not find that tool good enough, I skip the automated things. Maybe the best approach, I often try to find some tools that do not refactor my code completely. I try to find the parts that I have to, or that I may modify, and I check the results. And then, if there are things that I am thinking that it would be good to modify, I use them to modify it. In other cases, I do not. As you all know, these tools are very noisy. And of course, I wrote an automated tool to evolve the code or devolve the code. Maybe that’s it.

Concepts: Tooling Constraints and Control Mechanisms
SubCategories: Tooling Support for Source Code Rejuvenation

Quotation:
W: Have you ever used a tool to evolve your code?

T: Yes. I used the migration tool from Python 2 to Python 3, but didn’t resume my work from there. I’ve used tools of code evolution in C++, a long time ago, to evolve the framework being used, Qt. When it went from version 3 to 4, there was a migration tool because there were a lot of compatibility breaks. This tool is really great, it leaves almost nothing back. These two were command line utilitarians.

W: Regarding code evolving tools: That takes a legacy code and refactor into a new one, these tools could also do this, right?

T: Yes, exactly. This Python 2 to Python 3 one takes Python 2 code and automatically refactors into analog language constructions in Python 3. The Qt 3 to Qt 4 one also did this, but in this case it wasn’t the language constructions, it was API changes, many times not trivial changes, but traits from an older API, that was a class and turned into a collaboration between many classes and vice versa, and it performed these transformations.

SubCategories: Tooling Support for Source Code Rejuvenation
